rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
AI coding made my backlog explode.

My current rule: if I cannot explain who feels the pain, I should not open the editor yet.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
A solo builder can now create too many products too quickly.

Clarity became the scarce resource, not code.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:

Clarity became the scarce resource, not code.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
The first product filter I now trust: would I still write about this problem if I never linked the product?

If the answer is no, the idea is probably not strong enough yet.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
The dangerous part of agentic coding is not low-quality code.

I learned to slow down before product direction, not after implementation.

I learned to slow down before product direction, not after implementation.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
MD+HTML Reader started in my head as a Markdown and HTML reader.

The job is not reading files; it is regaining context.

rows.name: Ahab Wang

The job is not reading files; it is regaining context.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
When I built MD+HTML Reader, the most practical feature was not a fancy AI layer.

Boring workflows often hide real pain.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:
When I built MD+HTML Reader, the most practical feature was not a fancy AI layer.

Boring workflows often hide real pain.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
When I built MD+HTML Reader, the most practical feature was not a fancy AI layer.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content: I added recent browsing and recent modified lists to MD+HTML Reader because my own AI-generated docs kept disappearing into project folders.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content: I added recent browsing and recent modified lists to MD+HTML Reader because my own AI-generated docs kept disappearing into project folders.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
MD+HTML Reader skips node_modules, .git, dist, build, and other heavy folders.

A tool that scans real projects must respect the mess users actually have, not the neat demo folder.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
HTML preview sounded simple until I treated generated HTML as an artifact, not a web page.

Default safety is a product decision.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content: Mermaid support in MD+HTML Reader came from a real pain: agents love producing diagrams, but broken diagrams are hard to review inside raw Markdown.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
I had to inline local CSS for HTML previews because many AI artifacts are split across HTML and nearby CSS files.

A product feels better when it handles the files users actually get, not only the easiest format.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
A small boundary I learned from MD+HTML Reader: do not turn every reader into an editor.

Adding editing would have made the scope bigger without proving the core pain.

rows.content: My macOS release pipeline broke in ordinary ways: package manager mismatch, Tauri target arguments, stable DMG names, and signed update artifacts.

rows.content:
One Seek commit fixed a packaged app that hung on launch.

I now treat packaged-app testing as a separate product surface, not a final checkbox.

rows.content:
I spent time stripping macOS metadata from an updater archive.

Release engineering is full of tiny details that users only notice when you get them wrong.

rows.content:
MD+HTML Reader version bumps have to stay consistent across package.json, Tauri config, Cargo files, release notes, and update manifests.

Every repeated release step is a future script.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
I connected Creem billing to MD+HTML Reader before I had strong distribution.

It forced me to define plans, trial rules, license recovery, and what the product is actually worth.

rows.content:

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
MD+HTML Reader started with a 7-day trial and later moved toward a 30-day trial.

A local workflow tool may need enough time to become part of the user's habit.

Early products still need boring operational hygiene.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
Analytics felt premature until I asked one question: how will I know if a stranger actually opened the app tomorrow?

For MD+HTML Reader and Paste Switch, DAU-style events were less vanity and more reality check.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
I added app_environment to analytics because mixing test and live data makes tiny products lie to you.

With low traffic, one noisy internal session can distort the whole story.

Trust also comes from basic product quality.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
PostHog and GA were not magic dashboards for Seek.

They were a way to answer plain questions: did people land, did they click download, did they return, and did they reach a payment path?

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content: SEO work became real for me when I stopped saying search traffic and started fixing pages: sitemap, canonical paths, structured data, localized URLs, blog indexes, and Search Console verification.

rows.content:
A timezone bug in blog structured data was not glamorous, but it mattered.

If metadata is wrong, content can be good and still be hard for machines to trust.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
IndieSeek now has SEO drafts, blog pages, product pages, tools, and sitemaps.

The lesson: content strategy becomes more useful when it has a build pipeline, not just a Notion list.

rows.content:
I separated SEO blog work from founder-journey social posts.

Mixing them too early makes both weaker.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
Paste Switch looked like a tiny clipboard tool.

If the shortcut is not faster than muscle memory, the product is not done.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:

If the shortcut is not faster than muscle memory, the product is not done.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
Paste Switch looked like a tiny clipboard tool.

If the shortcut is not faster than muscle memory, the product is not done.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
I chose Cmd+Shift+V for Paste Switch, then had to respect that some apps already use it for paste-and-match-style.

It is negotiating with existing user habits.

rows.content:

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
Paste Switch needs macOS Accessibility permission to paste and undo.

That forced a product lesson: if your app depends on a scary permission, the onboarding has to explain the value before asking for trust.

rows.content:

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
One Paste Switch bug was about caret overlay positioning.

It was not a business insight, but it was a product insight: tiny UI details matter most in tools that live inside another app's workflow.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
Paste Switch v0.1.1 and v0.1.2 were mostly release hardening and installing state fixes.

They are about removing reasons a stranger would quit in the first minute.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
I kept Paste Switch clipboard history in memory instead of building a full database.

For an MVP, fewer promises can be better than a large feature set with unclear trust.

rows.content:
Paste Switch supports text and images, not every clipboard format.

A product gets clearer when it says no to adjacent use cases.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
A status-bar app sounds simple until you handle hidden Dock icons, focus loss, panels, permissions, shortcuts, and exit behavior.

Missing one makes the app feel amateur.

rows.content:
Preparing Paste Switch for Product Hunt forced a useful question: would a stranger understand the tool from one screenshot and one sentence?

It is positioning.

rows.content:
Adding a Product Hunt badge was easy.

A launch asset is not distribution by itself; it only amplifies whatever clarity already exists.

rows.content:
I started a retrospective draft about a weak Product Hunt launch because failure is more reusable than a polished announcement.

The useful part is not the product name; it is why the launch did not create enough pull.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
Product screenshots are strategy in disguise.

They are the fastest proof that a tool solves a real workflow, not a vague promise.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:
Product screenshots are strategy in disguise.

They are the fastest proof that a tool solves a real workflow, not a vague promise.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:

They are the fastest proof that a tool solves a real workflow, not a vague promise.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
ReplayFlow began as a recording tool, but the real question was: can a messy browser and desktop workflow become a reusable AI routine?

That is more interesting than just recording clicks.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:
ReplayFlow began as a recording tool, but the real question was: can a messy browser and desktop workflow become a reusable AI routine?

That is more interesting than just recording clicks.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
ReplayFlow began as a recording tool, but the real question was: can a messy browser and desktop workflow become a reusable AI routine?

That is more interesting than just recording clicks.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
The ReplayFlow app is intentionally a small floating recorder, not a dashboard.

That choice came from the workflow: when users are recording, the tool should get out of the way.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:
The ReplayFlow app is intentionally a small floating recorder, not a dashboard.

That choice came from the workflow: when users are recording, the tool should get out of the way.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
The ReplayFlow app is intentionally a small floating recorder, not a dashboard.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
ReplayFlow's browser extension captures navigation, clicks, input metadata, DOM hints, and before/after context.

The lesson: useful automation needs evidence, not just a video of what happened.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:

The lesson: useful automation needs evidence, not just a video of what happened.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:

The lesson: useful automation needs evidence, not just a video of what happened.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
ReplayFlow redacts password-like fields before sending events.

I learned this the practical way: any recorder that helps with real work must treat privacy as a core feature, not a settings page.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:
ReplayFlow redacts password-like fields before sending events.

I learned this the practical way: any recorder that helps with real work must treat privacy as a core feature, not a settings page.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
Desktop capture in ReplayFlow depends on macOS permissions and frontmost-app context.

That made the product boundary clearer: automation tools must tell users what they can verify and what still needs human judgment.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:
Desktop capture in ReplayFlow depends on macOS permissions and frontmost-app context.

That made the product boundary clearer: automation tools must tell users what they can verify and what still needs human judgment.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
ReplayFlow exports routines to formats like Codex skills, JSON, guides, and shortcut prompts.

That changed my thinking: the output of a tool can be a reusable workflow asset, not just a saved session.

rows.content:

rows.content:
I kept ReplayFlow isolated from the MD+HTML Reader app even though both are Tauri macOS apps.

Duplication can be cheaper than premature sharing when two products are still searching for their real shape.

Coverage matters less than useful reconstruction.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
Seek became a monorepo because web pages, macOS apps, docs, analytics, and release scripts needed to evolve together.

The benefit is that product experiments can reuse a distribution system.

rows.name: Ahab Wang

The benefit is that product experiments can reuse a distribution system.

rows.name: Ahab at IndieSeek
rows.content:
The IndieSeek main site taught me a product lesson I did not expect: a portfolio of tools needs taxonomy.

Without clear app pages, tools, blog pages, and pricing paths, the site becomes a drawer, not a storefront.

rows.name: Ahab Wang
rows.content:
The IndieSeek main site taught me a product lesson I did not expect: a portfolio of tools needs taxonomy.

Without clear app pages, tools, blog pages, and pricing paths, the site becomes a drawer, not a storefront.

Setting up Postiz for IndieSeek taught me that publishing automation has many unglamorous blockers: OAuth callbacks, app IDs, review scopes, pages, business portfolios, and API limits.

The calendar is the easy part.

Social integrations often fail at the exact place where documentation, app config, and local callbacks meet.

Product pages, pricing pages, privacy pages, release notes, analytics, and onboarding all become part of the product the moment strangers can see it.
